questions.q: The guide's definition: an operating system is...
options: a set of programs that manages the available resources of a computer
the program that draws windows and menus on the screen
the collection of all software installed on a machine
the firmware that starts the computer before anything else
questions.why: Linux, UNIX, BSD, Windows and MacOS are all operating systems by this definition.

questions.q: What is physical memory made up of?
options: The RAM bars and the processor cache memory
The hard disk and the swap partition
The RAM bars and the hard disk
The processor registers only
questions.why: Physical memory = RAM + processor cache, used for program execution.

options: On the hard disk, in the swap partition
In the processor cache
In a reserved area of the RAM
On the network, in a shared memory server
questions.why: The swap partition unloads physical memory and can save system state at electrical shutdown.

questions.q: Which of these is NOT one of the OS's resource-management jobs listed by the guide?
options: Translate source code into machine code
Intercept access to peripherals
Schedule processes to occupy the processor
Protect files from unauthorized access
questions.why: Compiling is a developer tool's job. The OS manages memory, peripherals, tasks, file protection, and collects information about programs in use.

questions.q: In the guide's layer diagram, what sits directly between applications and the hardware?
options: The operating system
The user
The shell
The compiler

questions.q: MULTICS (1964-1968) was developed for which three organisations?
options: MIT, Bell Labs (AT&T) and General Electric
IBM, DEC and Xerox
Berkeley, Stanford and MIT
AT&T, IBM and Microsoft
questions.why: MULTiplexed Information and Computing Service - the project UNIX later reacted against.
